retrieved_docs.title: Peanut Allergy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors include: History of atopy Family history of peanut allergy Maternal consumption of peanuts during pregnancy Use of certain oils to fry/roast peanuts

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Previous guidelines recommended delaying peanut introduction until after 12 months to prevent peanut allergy. However, recent studies show that introducing peanuts as early as 4 to 6 months of age can reduce the risk of developing a peanut allergy and is now recommended for most infants, particularly those at high risk. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Peanut Allergy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several studies have tested age-related peanut exposure in the development of peanut allergy. One study evaluated the prevalence of peanut allergy in Israeli Jewish children compared to Jewish children living in the United Kingdom (UK). Peanut introduction differs in these two populations whereby peanut-containing food is not restricted in Israel while it is typically delayed until the age of 2 in the UK. The results showed that Israeli children have a decreased frequency of developing a peanut allergy compared to the UK group, suggesting that the early introduction of peanuts may offer protection from developing a peanut allergy. [3] Another important study, the Learning Early About Peanut Allergy (LEAP) study, was a prospective randomized trial with one group avoiding peanuts until age 5 while the other group had repeated exposure to peanuts from 4 to 6 months until age 5. The purpose of the study was to compare the incidence of peanut allergy between the two groups after 5 years. The children in this study were at high risk of developing a peanut allergy. They defined high risk as either having severe eczema or egg allergy. The results of the LEAP study showed that early introduction of peanuts to high-risk infants early could prevent the development of a peanut allergy. [4] [5] Current guidelines recommend peanut introduction as early as 4 to 6 months of age in high-risk infants.

retrieved_docs.title: Protein Intolerance -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In an Australian study, one in 10,000 infants less than 2 years of age were found to have food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome (FPIES). FPIES is found to occur predominantly in infants and is associated with common food triggers (proteins in cow milk, soy, rice, oats, and egg), but the epidemiological data are still lacking. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Environmental factors play a critical role in modulating immune function and influencing the risk of food allergy development. Factors such as mode of delivery (cesarean section versus vaginal delivery), early antibiotic exposure, and alterations in the gut microbiome can influence immune tolerance and the integrity of the intestinal barrier. [4] Children delivered via cesarean section face a higher risk of developing food allergies, possibly related to the lack of exposure to beneficial microbes encountered during vaginal delivery. [4] Early antibiotic use in childhood may increase the risk of developing food allergies, likely due to disruption of the gut microbiome and impaired immune regulation. [5] Additionally, the timing of introducing potentially allergenic solid foods to infants plays a significant role in the risk of food allergies, a topic further explored in the Deterrence and Patient Education section below.

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Non-IgE–mediated food allergies, including irritability, vomiting or frequent spitting up, diarrhea, and poor weight gain, commonly present with gastrointestinal symptoms in infants and young children. In FPIES, affected infants typically experience vomiting 1 to 3 hours after ingestion of the triggering food. With continued exposure, symptoms may progress to include abdominal distention, bloody diarrhea, lethargy, anemia, and failure to thrive. The most frequently implicated foods in FPIES include cow's milk, soy, oats, and rice.

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: About 6% of children experience food allergic reactions in the first three years of life, including approximately 2.5% with cow’s milk allergy, 1.5% with egg allergy, and 1% with peanut allergy. Studies have shown that peanut allergy prevalence increased over the past decade. Most children tend to outgrow milk and egg allergies by school-age. In contrast, children with peanut, nut, or seafood allergy retain their allergy for life. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Egg Allergy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma has often been associated with food allergies due to the association with IgE mediated reactions.  A small cohort study involving 1218 children indicated an incidence of asthma in 80% of the children with egg allergy with a calculated odds ratio of 5.0 [5] .

retrieved_docs.title: Cow Milk Allergy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Children who display an allergy to milk are more likely to develop other allergies to foods. Associated adverse reactions to different foods develop in up to 50% of children, and allergies against inhalants occur in 50% to 80% before puberty. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Wheat Allergy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of confirmed (via food challenge), IgE-mediated food allergy to wheat is unknown. The prevalence of wheat-based food allergy is likely in the range of 0.2% to 1%. [1] Children have a higher prevalence of wheat allergies compared to adults and are more likely to develop an allergy if wheat is introduced after six months of life. [1] [4] Children typically outgrow their allergy, and about 65% have a resolution by the age of 12. [5] The prevalence of celiac disease, a non-IgE-mediated food allergy to wheat, is approximately 1% to 2% in the United States. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies (Nursing) -- Evidence-Based Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of infants and young children develop tolerance to their food allergies with time. Most children outgrow their allergies to eggs, milk, and soy within 3-5 years. However, there are also reports that more than 50% of children will continue to have food allergies that persist to puberty. However, over time, even these children will develop tolerance to their allergies by the end of the second decade of life. Children who have non-IgE mediated food allergies such as enterocolitis usually have a cessation of their disorder within the first few years after birth.  Unfortunately, children with eosinophilic esophagitis may have symptoms that continue to persist. Severe anaphylactic reactions with food allergies are rare but do occur. The fatalities are usually seen in school children and the foods implicated include shellfish, peanuts, and fish. [14] [15] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Wheat Allergy -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Peanut Allergy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Food allergies affect approximately 4% to 8% of children and 1% to 2% of adults. [1] Peanut allergy typically manifests initially during childhood with symptom occurrence as early as 4 months of age and usually within the first 2 years of life. Approximately 20% of children will naturally outgrow their peanut allergy and tolerate them without events later on in life.

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies (Nursing) -- Medical Management -- Prevention of Food Allergy
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, the recommendations are to introduce complementary solid foods, such as egg, peanut products, fish, wheat, and other allergenic foods one at a time after four to six months of age when breastfeeding, as there is no need to avoid or delay their introduction.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Etiology -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for asthma development encompass exposures throughout a patient's lifespan, including the perinatal period. The most substantial known risk factor is atopy, which is characterized by the genetic tendency to produce specific immunoglobulin E (IgE) antibodies in response to common environmental allergens. Nearly one-third of children with atopy will develop asthma later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Although genetic predisposition contributes to the development of food allergies, adopting specific dietary strategies may help reduce the risk in young children. Exclusive breastfeeding has a protective effect, likely because breast milk contains essential immune factors that support the maturation of the infant’s immune system. This may reduce the risk of developing food allergies, as well as other allergic conditions such as atopic dermatitis and asthma. The AAP recommends exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months of life. [40] Additionally, current evidence does not support avoiding potentially allergenic foods during pregnancy or lactation as a strategy to prevent the development of food allergies in infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
retrieved_docs.content: of these conditions and risk factors for clinicians monitoring both maternal health and pediatric safety. Collectively, the relationships suggest that hormonal prophylaxis, careful assessment of obstetric history, management of maternal asthma, and mitigation of environmental pollutants are important components of comprehensive care aimed at reducing spontaneous abortion and its downstream neonatal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2316
retrieved_docs.content: A poor reproductive history, reflecting previous obstetric complications or adverse outcomes, is clinically associated with an increased risk of preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This connection highlights the importance of detailed reproductive assessments in prenatal care to identify women who may be more likely to experience early delivery and to apply targeted monitoring and preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) are situated within the broader category of Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation, which is classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}. Within the perinatal‑originating conditions, there is a more specific subgroup of Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). This hierarchical organization indicates that infants born with extreme prematurity and unspecified low birth weight are at risk for a range of perinatal conditions, including distinct respiratory and cardiovascular disorders, highlighting the importance of recognizing the underlying gestational and growth‑related factors when assessing and managing newborn health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 552
retrieved_docs.content: avoiding contraindicated drugs in reflux; and maintaining vigilance for serious pediatric adverse events—including tachycardia, umbilical cord prolapse, and neonatal mortality—especially in the context of preterm low birth weight and its long‑term growth implications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 92
retrieved_docs.content: constitute an integrated, multilingual framework for comprehensive risk assessment, diagnosis and management of normal and complicated pregnancy, labor, delivery, immediate newborn care, and related systemic diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.
